full_result.sentinel_validation: Three issues were identified and corrected across the queries. (1) ELSEVIER: Inside the first concept block, 'heat island AND public health' was embedded within an OR group without its own parentheses, causing a precedence error — 'heat island' was moved to concept block 4 as a standalone OR term and 'heat-health warning' was added for consistency with PubMed. The same pattern error affected 'emergency department AND heat', 'cardiovascular AND heat', and 'respiratory AND heat' in block 2 — these were replaced with properly phrased compound terms matching PubMed equivalents. 'causal inference' and 'program evaluation' were added to block 3 for consistency. (2) PUBMED: Query was already well-structured and syntactically valid; no changes were required. (3) OPENALEX: Critical operator precedence errors were found throughout — AND operators were chained across long OR lists without parentheses, meaning only the last OR term before each AND was being intersected rather than the entire concept block. All four concept blocks were wrapped in parentheses to enforce correct grouping. 'heat warning' and 'heat-health warning' were added to block 1, and 'impact evaluation' was added to block 3 for alignment across databases.
sentinel_warnings: PubMed: MeSH terms (e.g., 'Heat Stress Disorders'[MeSH], 'Early Warning Systems'[MeSH], 'Mortality'[MeSH]) are not included. Adding MeSH terms in a separate parallel search string is recommended per Cochrane standards to improve recall for indexed biomedical literature.
ELSEVIER/PUBMED: The term 'effectiv*' (Scopus) and 'effectiveness' (PubMed) are broadly generic and may introduce noise. Monitor precision metrics during screening.

OPENALEX: OpenAlex has limited support for field-tag restrictions (e.g., Title/Abstract filtering). The query relies on relevance ranking rather than field-scoped searching, which may reduce precision compared to Scopus and PubMed versions.

All databases: 'heat island' as a standalone term without 'public health' qualifier (as removed from Elsevier block 1) may still retrieve urban planning literature unrelated to health interventions — consider monitoring or removing if precision is low.

(2) The broad terms 'effectiv*' (Elsevier) and 'effectiveness' (PubMed/OpenAlex) are retained in block 3 (methodological terms) where they are constrained by intersection with three other specific concept blocks, substantially limiting noise; a note is preserved in refinement_notes for screeners to monitor precision. full_result.formulator_reasoning: The query is structured around four core conceptual pillars connected by AND, each grouped with OR synonyms to maximize recall without sacrificing too much precision. Pillar 1 (Intervention/Exposure): captures the heat warning and alert system terminology — the primary intervention of interest — using both hyphenated and unhyphenated variants (e.g., 'heat-health action plan' vs 'heat health action plan'), and includes MeSH 'Heat Wave' in PubMed for biomedical indexing coverage. Pillar 2 (Context/Setting): anchors the search to heat-related public health and climate adaptation contexts, including 'cooling center', 'urban heat', and 'vulnerable population' to filter out unrelated alert system studies (e.g., tsunami, earthquake warnings). Pillar 3 (Health Outcomes): covers the full spectrum of relevant outcomes — mortality subtypes (cardiovascular, respiratory, heat-related, excess), morbidity indicators (ED visits, hospitalizations, heat stroke), with PubMed leveraging MeSH terms for 'Emergency Service, Hospital' and 'Cause of Death' to improve biomedical recall. Pillar 4 (Causal/Quasi-experimental Methods): restricts results to evaluative studies using credible causal identification strategies — DiD, RD, ITS, natural experiments, IV — ensuring methodological rigor appropriate for a systematic review of policy effectiveness. OpenAlex uses a flatter syntax as that platform handles proximity and relevance internally, so OR chains are used across concepts. Key trade-off: including broad terms like 'mortality' and 'morbidity' in Pillar 3 increases sensitivity but may inflate noise; post-hoc title/abstract screening is recommended. The Pillar 2 context filter is the primary specificity lever to reduce off-topic alert system literature.

Parentheses are balanced, TITLE-ABS-KEY wrapper is properly applied, wildcards are used appropriately (hospitaliz*, heat-related death*, vulnerable population*), and all four conceptual blocks (intervention, context/population, outcome, study design) are correctly connected with AND and internally grouped with OR. sentinel_warnings: OpenAlex does not support field-level tags (e.g., Title/Abstract restrictions) or wildcards in the same way as Scopus or PubMed. The simplified flat-text query is appropriate for OpenAlex's default search behavior, but results will be broader and less precise than the Scopus/PubMed equivalents.

The 'early warning system' term in the intervention block is broad and may retrieve studies unrelated to heat (e.g., flood, earthquake, or disease outbreak warning systems). Consider monitoring false-positive rates during title/abstract screening.
The 'public health alert' term in Scopus and the absence of it in OpenAlex may introduce minor cross-database inconsistency. This is an acceptable trade-off given OpenAlex's simpler query engine.

MeSH term 'Heat Stress Disorders' in PubMed covers heatstroke, heat exhaustion, and related conditions, which may slightly expand the outcome block beyond the intended scope. This is generally beneficial for sensitivity but should be noted.

The second conceptual block (context/population) functions as a thematic filter rather than a strict requirement — some highly relevant studies (e.g., pure mortality time-series analyses) may not mention cooling centers or urban heat explicitly, potentially causing false negatives. Reviewers should assess whether this block should be relaxed or made optional depending on desired sensitivity.

full_result.refiner_notes: Five warnings from the validation report were systematically addressed. (1) The overly broad 'early warning system' term was replaced with the more specific 'heat early warning' across all three databases, eliminating false positives from flood, earthquake, and disease outbreak warning literature while retaining sensitivity for heat-specific alerts. (2) The second conceptual block (context/population) was substantially expanded in all three databases with terms 'extreme heat', 'high temperature', 'heat exposure', 'ambient temperature', 'heatwave', and 'heat wave' — this ensures that pure mortality time-series analyses and heat exposure studies that do not mention cooling centers or urban heat are no longer excluded as false negatives, directly resolving the sensitivity concern. (3) Cross-database consistency was improved by adding 'public health alert' back into the OpenAlex query and aligning synonym lists across all three platforms as closely as the respective engines allow. (4) The PubMed MeSH expansion risk from 'Heat Stress Disorders' is mitigated by the now-broader outcome block which ensures the MeSH term does not disproportionately inflate one block relative to others; the term is retained for recall value. (5) Two additional methodological terms — 'counterfactual' and 'synthetic control' — were added to the study design block in all three databases to improve coverage of modern causal inference literature. The Elsevier and PubMed queries retain their existing wildcard and field-tag structures. The OpenAlex query remains field-tag-free and wildcard-free in accordance with that engine's query syntax limitations, but is now more comprehensive in synonym coverage. All parentheses are balanced, Boolean hierarchies are correct, and all three queries are ready for immediate execution.

full_result.pulse_reasoning: Variation 4 (disciplinary cross-perspective mix): This expansion blends biometeorology, health geography, climate services, and causal inference vocabularies to capture research that may not self-identify as 'heat warning' literature. Key additions include biometeorological and climate-services terminology ('biometeorological advisory', 'climate service intervention', 'meteorological threshold policy') to capture interdisciplinary work at the weather-health interface. Physiological and epidemiological framing ('thermoregulatory public intervention', 'thermal environment morbidity', 'heat-attributable burden') broadens retrieval across public health and environmental epidemiology literatures. Causal inference terms are rephrased from standard vocabulary ('counterfactual heat analysis', 'treatment effect heterogeneity', 'policy discontinuity design') to capture methods-focused papers that may use non-standard phrasing. Operational terms like 'forecasting lead time effect' and 'early warning system efficacy' target program evaluation literature, while 'syndromic surveillance heat response' and 'preventive health mobilization' address public health systems research that evaluates forecast-activated protocols. full_result.formulator_reasoning: The query is structured around four core concept pillars that must co-occur to identify rigorous causal evaluations of heat warning/alert systems on health outcomes. Pillar 1 (Intervention): captures the full spectrum of heat warning typologies — system-level alerts, action plans, advisory communications, and emergency responses — including both 'heat wave' and 'heatwave' spelling variants to avoid missing British/Australian literature. Pillar 2 (Mechanism/Program component): targets specific public health response measures like cooling centers and preparedness programs, which are the operational expressions of heat warning systems and help sharpen precision beyond general climatology papers. Pillar 3 (Health Outcomes): covers the primary endpoints of interest — mortality (all-cause, cardiovascular, excess), morbidity (respiratory, ED visits, hospitalization), and heat-specific illness — using both free-text and MeSH terms in PubMed for maximum recall on biomedical literature. Pillar 4 (Causal/Quasi-experimental Methods): is strategically critical — it filters for studies attempting causal identification using RDD, ITS, DiD, natural experiments, or IV designs, which directly addresses the systematic review's focus on policy effectiveness evidence rather than descriptive epidemiology. The four-pillar AND structure prioritizes specificity over raw recall, reflecting the niche intersection of health policy evaluation and climate epidemiology. In PubMed, MeSH terms supplement free-text for Pillars 3 to catch indexed studies using different terminology. OpenAlex uses simplified syntax without field tags since its internal relevance ranking handles field weighting. Across all databases, synonym expansion within each pillar uses OR to maximize sensitivity within each concept domain.

full_result.sentinel_validation: All three queries passed structural and syntax validation. Key refinements applied across all databases: (1) CONCEPT BLOCK 1 — Added 'heat advisory' and aligned 'early warning system' and 'heat alert system' consistently across all three queries for parity; (2) CONCEPT BLOCK 2 — Added 'heat stress prevention' and 'heat protection' (Scopus only) to reduce risk of missing upstream prevention-focused studies; added 'hot weather' as a PubMed Title/Abstract term and aligned the MeSH tag 'Heat Wave' as a supplementary PubMed anchor; (3) CONCEPT BLOCK 3 — Added 'heat illness' as a broader synonym for heat-related illness; ensured wildcard truncation is only used in Scopus/Elsevier (e.g., 'mortalit*', 'morbid*', 'hospitalization*') since PubMed and OpenAlex handle wildcards differently or not at all in phrase-quoted contexts; (4) CONCEPT BLOCK 4 — Added 'synthetic control' and 'propensity score' as additional causal/quasi-experimental design terms to improve sensitivity for evaluative studies; (5) Parentheses checked and confirmed balanced in all three queries; (6) All Boolean operators confirmed uppercase; (7) PubMed MeSH tags verified for correct bracketed format; (8) Scopus TITLE-ABS-KEY wrapper confirmed correctly opened and closed.
sentinel_warnings: The 'early warning system' term in Concept Block 1 is broad and may retrieve studies on non-heat warning systems (e.g., infectious disease, flood, or disaster early warning). Consider monitoring recall-precision tradeoff and applying post-hoc title/abstract screening filters.

PubMed's 'Mortality'[MeSH Terms] is a very broad MeSH heading and may inflate result counts substantially. If precision is prioritized, consider replacing with more specific MeSH terms such as 'Cause of Death'[MeSH Terms] or restricting to 'Heat Stroke'[MeSH Terms] alone.

OpenAlex does not natively support MeSH terms or field-tag restrictions like [Title/Abstract]. The OpenAlex query relies on full-text or metadata matching, which may reduce precision compared to PubMed and Scopus. Consider supplementing with OpenAlex concept filters (e.g., C2779134260 for 'heat wave') if using the API directly.

Wildcard truncation (e.g., 'mortalit*', 'morbid*') is applied only in the Scopus query as intended. Confirm that the target PubMed interface supports or auto-handles pluralization, as PubMed's automatic term mapping may not always expand quoted phrase searches.

full_result.refiner_notes: Four warnings from the validation report were fully addressed: (1) The broad 'early warning system' term was REMOVED from all three queries across Concept Block 1, eliminating the primary source of off-topic retrieval (flood, disease, disaster warning studies) while retaining all genuinely heat-specific warning terms; this is the single most impactful precision improvement. (2) The overbroad 'Mortality'[MeSH Terms] heading in PubMed was REPLACED with the more specific 'Cause of Death'[MeSH Terms], substantially reducing result set inflation while preserving mortality-related sensitivity alongside the retained heat-specific title/abstract terms and 'Heat Stroke'[MeSH Terms]. (3) The OpenAlex query was confirmed to use no field-tag syntax, relying entirely on phrase matching against full-text and metadata as appropriate for that platform; 'difference in differences' (unquoted variant) was added alongside the hyphenated form for broader matching given OpenAlex's full-text indexing behavior. (4) Wildcard truncation (mortalit*, morbid*, death*, hospitalization*, visit*) is retained exclusively in the Scopus/Elsevier query; PubMed and OpenAlex queries use explicit full-form phrases only, avoiding any ambiguity around quoted wildcard handling. Additional structural improvements: 'weather forecast intervention' and 'temperature forecast advisory' were restored to all three queries for full parity; 'hot weather health' was added to Elsevier Block 2 as a more specific variant of the broad 'hot weather' term used in PubMed and OpenAlex; outer parentheses nesting in PubMed was corrected to use double outer brackets for cleaner AND-block separation; all Boolean operators confirmed uppercase throughout; all parentheses confirmed balanced across all three queries.
